title: Israel May Stand Alone vs Iran
summary: Chuck Freilich describes the circumstances under which Israel may pay the price for an overly hasty deal with Iran.
body: This is an English-language summary of a Hebrew-language op-ed that appeared in Ynet on July 9, 2019. This summary was provided by the author. The latest from Belfer See more With Iran's announcement that it has now begun exceeding the quantity and level of highly enriched uranium it is permitted under the nuclear deal, Trump's policy of "maximal pressure" has reached a critical turning point. U.S. sanctions have caused a severe economic crisis in Iran, but the objective is a change in Iranian policy, not just to cause pain, and so far there are no signs of that, to the contrary. Iran may be the first to blink in the escalatory cycle with the United States, but this is far from assured. Trump has made it abundantly clear that he does not wish a further military conflict in the Middle East, especially during an election year, but is also publicly committed to preventing Iran from going nuclear. Iranian leaders, more convinced than ever of the strategic imperative of a nuclear capability, cannot allow themselves to appear as the side that simply caved in. If a "better" deal is to be achieved, it will have to be better for Iran too. In these circumstances, Israel may find itself alone in the military campaign, but the diplomatic route holds out dangers as well. Much as Trump "fell in love" with the leader of North Korea in their very first meeting, he may develop similarly strong feelings for Supreme Leader, Khamenai. Israel may end up paying the price for an overly hasty deal. This is particularly risk fraught at a time when Israel is caught up in its own elections, and its leader is fighting not just for his political future, but very freedom. Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. Recommended citation Freilich, Chuck."Israel May Stand Alone vs Iran." Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School, July 23, 2019. result.main_content_en: The article analyzes the critical juncture reached by the U.S. 'maximum pressure' policy following Iran's announcement of exceeding nuclear deal enrichment limits. While sanctions have caused a severe economic crisis in Iran, the intended policy change has not materialized. The author assesses the escalatory cycle between the U.S. and Iran, noting President Trump's aversion to new military conflict, especially during an election year, alongside his commitment to preventing a nuclear Iran. Iranian leaders, however, are seen as more convinced of the strategic need for nuclear capability and cannot afford to appear as having capitulated. The analysis concludes that any new deal would need to offer benefits for Iran. In this precarious situation, Israel risks finding itself isolated in a potential military campaign. Furthermore, a diplomatic route is also perilous, as a rapid U.S.-Iran deal, possibly mirroring Trump's approach to North Korea, could come at Israel's expense. This risk is heightened during Israel's own election period, where its leader is embroiled in a high-stakes political and legal battle.
result.main_content_zh: 本文分析了伊朗宣布突破核协议浓缩铀限制后，美国“极限施压”政策所达到的关键转折点。尽管制裁已导致伊朗陷入严重经济危机，但预期的伊朗政策改变并未出现。作者评估了美伊之间的升级循环，指出特朗普总统一方面不愿在中东引发新的军事冲突（尤其是在选举年），另一方面又公开承诺阻止伊朗拥核。然而，伊朗领导人被认为比以往任何时候都更坚信拥有核能力的战略必要性，且不能让自己显得轻易屈服。分析认为，任何新协议也必须对伊朗有利。在这种不稳定局势下，以色列可能发现自己在潜在的军事行动中陷入孤立。此外，外交途径同样危险，因为美伊之间可能仓促达成协议（类似特朗普对朝鲜的做法），而让以色列付出代价。在以色列自身处于选举期、其领导人正为政治前途乃至个人自由而战的背景下，这一风险尤为突出。
